title: Introduction
text: Traditional fraud detection often focuses on individual transactions. But when it comes to organized fraud, we're dealing with coordinated networks—multiple actors working together. This is where graph algorithms really shine, as they can reveal these hidden groups. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s173-000-existing] Let's take a look at this graph of financial transactions. Here, you can see how individual nodes representing fraudsters are highlighted, showing how they form a network within the graph.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be equipped to identify fraud patterns that might be invisible in tables but become obvious in graphs. You'll explore a real fraud dataset to discover suspicious connections, design a graph projection strategy for fraud investigation, and choose appropriate algorithms to narrow the search space and rank suspects.

title: Why Graphs?
text: Fraudsters are quite adept at hiding their tracks. They often use multiple accounts, shared devices, and credit cards, as well as complex transaction chains. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s175-001-existing] As you can see in this illustration, these connections are invisible in tables but become clear in graphs. A relational database would require multiple JOINs to uncover what a single graph traversal can reveal instantly.

title: The Dataset
text: Let's put these principles into practice with a real fraud network. You'll be working with an anonymized peer-to-peer financial transactions dataset. This dataset includes User nodes, some of which are flagged as known fraudsters, along with Card, Device, and IP nodes. The relationships include P2P, HAS_CC, HAS_IP, and USED. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s176-001-new] Here's a schema diagram illustrating the dataset structure, showing how these nodes and relationships are interconnected.

title: Explore the Schema
text: To get a better understanding of the data model, you can run the following command: CALL db.schema.visualization(). Take a moment to grasp the structure. You'll notice that Users connect to Cards, Devices, and IPs, and also to other Users via P2P transactions.

title: Node and Relationship Counts
text: Let's check out the scale of our data. By running a query to count nodes and relationships, you'll find approximately 790,000 nodes and 1.8 million relationships. This is a vast search space, far too large for manual investigation. That's why we need algorithms to help us focus our efforts.

title: Fraud Flags
text: In our dataset, known fraudulent users are flagged with the property fraudMoneyTransfer = 1. You can run a query to return these users, limiting the results to the first 10.

title: Explore Flagged Users
text: After running the query, expand the nodes you find. Pay attention to how many unflagged users connect to each flagged user, the types of nodes like Cards, Devices, and IPs that appear in the neighborhood, and any shared infrastructure between flagged users. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s180-003-new] This graph diagram highlights connections and shared infrastructure, which are crucial for our investigation.

title: Transfer Chains
text: Now, let's explore how flagged users connect to each other through transaction chains. You can run a query to find paths between flagged users, limited to 100 results. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s181-004-new] This flowchart illustrates transaction chains between flagged users, showing intermediaries and potential money flow.

title: What the Chains Reveal
text: Notice that users in the middle of these chains often aren't flagged, while flagged users at either end suggest that the middle users might be involved. Money flows through these intermediaries, whether intentionally or not. Discovering this pattern in a relational database would require five self-JOINs on the transaction table. In a graph, it's a simple path query. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s182-005-new] This diagram compares the complexity of detecting patterns in relational databases versus graph databases, highlighting the simplicity of path queries in graphs.

title: Shared Infrastructure
text: When two known fraudsters share the same credit card, it's highly suspicious. You can run a query to find paths where flagged users share infrastructure like credit cards or devices. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s183-006-new] This illustration shows two fraudsters sharing a credit card or device, emphasizing the suspicious nature of shared infrastructure in fraud detection.

title: The Key Question
text: Finding connections between known fraudsters isn't particularly remarkable. The real question is: Can we use these patterns to find fraudsters we don't know about yet? That's exactly what this module will teach you.

title: The Challenge
text: With around 790,000 nodes and 1.8 million relationships, manual investigation is simply not feasible. We need algorithms to help us narrow the search space and find suspicious communities, as well as rank suspects to prioritize who to investigate first. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s185-007-new] This flowchart depicts the algorithmic approach to narrowing the search space and ranking suspects, illustrating the steps involved in the process.

title: Designing the Projection
text: Before we run any algorithms, we need to decide what to project. This network is heterogeneous, containing multiple node and relationship types, such as Users, Cards, Devices, and IPs, along with P2P transactions, HAS_CC, HAS_IP, and USED relationships. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s186-002-existing] This diagram shows User, Card, IP, and Device nodes connected by multiple relationships, illustrating the heterogeneous nature of the graph.

title: Projection Options
text: There are several ways we could project this network. A monopartite approach captures direct transactions but loses shared infrastructure. A bipartite approach captures shared infrastructure but loses direct transactions. A heterogeneous approach captures everything, although it's more complex.

title: Our Choice: Heterogeneous
text: For fraud detection, shared infrastructure is critical evidence. We'll project Users, Cards, and Devices together. This allows our algorithms to find communities based on all connection types, not just transactions. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s188-009-new] This diagram illustrates the heterogeneous projection of Users, Cards, and Devices, showing how this approach captures all connection types for fraud detection.

title: The Algorithmic Strategy
text: We'll use two algorithm families in sequence. First, community detection to find groups containing known fraudsters, and then centrality to rank users within those groups. Community detection reduces the search space, while centrality ranks the suspects. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s189-010-new] This flowchart shows the sequential use of Community Detection and Centrality algorithms, explaining how each step contributes to fraud detection.

title: Applying the Framework
text: Let's formalize our approach. Our key question is: Who else is involved in fraud networks? We'll project Users, Cards, and Devices with their relationships, using community detection followed by centrality ranking. Start with default configurations and refine based on results. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-1-fraud-problem-s190-011-new] This diagram summarizes the formalized approach to fraud detection, including the key question, projection strategy, algorithm choice, and configuration.

title: What's Next
text: In the following lessons, you'll dive deeper into the techniques. Lesson 2 covers how Louvain community detection works. In Lesson 3, you'll run Louvain to reduce your search space by 98%. Lesson 4 introduces Degree Centrality and WCC for formal community assignment. Finally, Lesson 5 will guide you in building fraud communities using entity resolution patterns. Each lesson builds on the previous one, taking you from raw data to actionable suspect lists.

title: Summary
text: Fraud detection is shifting from focusing on individual transactions to analyzing networks. The graph structure reveals connections that fraudsters try to hide. Community detection identifies fraud rings, and centrality ranks suspects within those rings. You've explored the dataset and seen how flagged users connect through transactions and shared infrastructure. Now, you're ready to apply algorithms to find the fraudsters hiding in plain sight.
